meta.purpose: Big-name AI opinions and hard-number external context for Analysis Camp slice 4 comparison cards
meta.note: Complements content/x-post-research.json (field-voice X posts). These are surveys, RCTs, and executive claims — never cohort voice.

searchNotes: First X pass targeted RaidGuild field voice and adjacent builders; it did not systematically search executive timelines or survey archives.
Hard numbers here come from primary reports (Stack Overflow, METR, HEPI, Gallup, Anthropic Institute) and on-record CEO statements (CNBC LlamaCon, Anthropic paper).
Stack Overflow 2026 Developer Survey is open (Jun 2026); 2025 data is the latest published archive.
Executive code-authorship claims (30%, 80%) are self-reported and vendor-skewed — label clearly as industry claims, not independent measurement.

signals.speaker: Stack Overflow Developer Survey
signals.headline: 84% of developers use or plan to use AI tools; 51% of professionals use them daily
signals.opinion: Adoption is mainstream; enthusiasm is cooling even as usage rises.
caveats: Global developer population, not RaidGuild cohort

signals.speaker: Stack Overflow Developer Survey
signals.headline: More developers distrust AI output accuracy (46%) than trust it (33%)
signals.quote: Only a fraction (3%) report highly trusting the output, indicating a widespread need for human verification for those in roles with accountability.
signals.opinion: Verification moved upstream in practice before it moved upstream in hype.

signals.speaker: Joel Becker et al.
signals.headline: Experienced OSS developers were 19% slower with AI tools in a randomized trial

metrics.label: Perceived speedup (after)
metrics.context: Developers still believed AI helped after experiencing slowdown

signals.quote: When developers are allowed to use AI tools, they take 19% longer to complete issues—a significant slowdown that goes against developer beliefs and expert forecasts.
signals.opinion: The perception–reality gap on productivity is itself a field finding worth citing next to cohort claims about review and judgment.
caveats: Early-2025 models/tools; METR notes Feb 2026 follow-up shows likely speedup now
OSS maintainers on familiar repos only

signals.speaker: Joel Becker et al.
signals.headline: Human code review became the bottleneck as agents spread; RCT design broke under selection

metrics.label: Developers avoiding no-AI arm
metrics.context: Chose not to submit some tasks if AI disallowed

metrics.label: Claude Code share of public GitHub commits
metrics.context: Cited by METR from SemiAnalysis newsletter

signals.quote: As Claude generates more code, human code review has become the constraint.
signals.opinion: Industry data now echoes the cohort fireside line: execution cheapens, review and gates do not.
caveats: Review bottleneck quote paraphrased from Anthropic Institute paper, echoed in METR update

signals.speaker: Joel Becker
signals.headline: Technical workers self-report median 1.4–2× value uplift; METR warns perceptions overstate reality
signals.quote: Our study in early 2025 found that people overestimated AI's effect on their time spent on tasks by 40 percentage points on average.
signals.opinion: Use as a skeptical counterweight to CEO code-share claims — big numbers, explicit methodology caveats.

signals.speaker: Satya Nadella
signals.headline: 20–30% of code in Microsoft repos is now written by software (AI)
signals.quote: I'd say maybe 20%, 30% of the code that is inside of our repos today and some of our projects are probably all written by software.
signals.opinion: Sets the executive ceiling on the 'code is cheap' narrative — pair with cohort review-gate warnings.

signals.speaker: Sundar Pichai
signals.headline: More than 25% of new code at Google is generated by AI
signals.opinion: Corroborates the big-tech code-generation race; cite alongside Nadella and Anthropic for a triad, not as ground truth.

signals.speaker: Mark Zuckerberg
signals.headline: Maybe half of Meta's Llama development could be done by AI within a year
signals.quote: Our bet is sort of that in the next year probably … maybe half the development is going to be done by AI, as opposed to people, and then that will just kind of increase from there.
signals.opinion: Forward-looking hype anchor — useful contrast to METR slowdown and Stack Overflow distrust.

signals.speaker: Anthropic Institute
signals.headline: 80%+ of Anthropic production code merged by Claude; human review is now the constraint

metrics.label: Automated reviewer bug catch rate
metrics.context: Would have caught past claude.ai incidents

signals.quote: As Claude generates more code, human code review has become the constraint.
signals.opinion: Strongest big-name package for the module thesis: volume up, verification gates tighten.
caveats: Paper also advocates global pause — cite selectively

signals.speaker: HEPI / Savanta
signals.headline: 95% of UK undergrads use AI; 12% paste AI text into assessed work (up from 3% in 2024)
signals.quote: Reflection papers used to proxy for understanding — AI makes those proxies unreliable.
signals.opinion: Hard education-pressure numbers that mirror Kerp's proxy-collapse framing; quote line is cohort paraphrase for pairing.

signals.speaker: Lumina Foundation–Gallup
signals.headline: 57% of U.S. college students use AI weekly; 53% say schools discourage or prohibit it
signals.quote: AI is already a routine part of college students' academic work, even as institutional guidance has not fully caught up.
signals.opinion: Policy–behavior gap supports Kerp + HEPI triangulation for education cards.

signals.speaker: Andrej Karpathy
signals.headline: Verifiability is the predictive feature — domains with reward functions get agentic leverage
signals.quote: In this new programming paradigm then, the new most predictive feature to look at is verifiability. If a task/job is verifiable, then it is optimizable directly or via reinforcement learning.
signals.opinion: Big-name framing that validates the cohort's verification-through-line without vendor spin.

signals.speaker: Andrej Karpathy
signals.headline: Coding agents 'basically didn't work before December' and 'basically work since'
signals.quote: Coding agents basically didn't work before December and basically work since — the models have significantly higher quality, long-term coherence and tenacity.
signals.opinion: Useful hype-counterweight timing marker; pair with METR selection-effects post from same winter.

cohortContrastPairs.cohortClaim: Execution got cheaper; taste, sequencing, and assessment got expensive
cohortContrastPairs.contrastNote: 84% adoption vs 46% distrust — industry data supports the bottleneck shift to judgment and QA.

cohortContrastPairs.cohortClaim: Agentic coding works better as doer/reviewer pipeline with deterministic checks
cohortContrastPairs.contrastNote: Anthropic reports 80% AI-authored merges but names human review as the constraint.

cohortContrastPairs.cohortClaim: Proxy collapse — assignments no longer prove understanding
cohortContrastPairs.contrastNote: 12% direct AI paste into assessed work; 65% say assessment already changed.

cohortContrastPairs.cohortClaim: Speed without review makes work lonelier and harder to verify
cohortContrastPairs.contrastNote: RCT found 19% slowdown while developers still felt 20% faster — perception gap is measurable.

gapsRemaining: No curated X posts yet from @sama, @gdb, @demis_hassabis, @drfeifei with hard numbers in-window — add in pass 2 via handle search.
Product-compression spend-vs-productivity stats (McKinsey, etc.) not yet sourced — needed for Graven/Bill contrast.
OSS security AI-review adoption rates still missing — Travis/open-source leg needs a cited survey.
